nameMeanings.name: Hedde
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Kurt, Claus, Eberhard, Hans, Juergen, Markus, Wolfgang.
German and Jewish: variant of   Heid  .
Dutch: rare variant of Van der Heide (see Vanderheide ) and, in North America, (also) a shortened form of this.

variants.name: Heidi
meanings: German: variant (and also an Americanized form, to retain the second syllable) of   Heide  .

variants.name: Heider
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Erwin, Otto, Fritz, Gunter, Johann, Konrad, Kurt, Matthias, Willibald.

German and Swiss German: topographic name for a heath dweller, a variant of Heide + the -er suffix denoting an inhabitant; or a habitational name for someone from any of various places called Heide or Heidau (named with the same term); the placename is common in Silesia, Saxony, and Bohemia.

variants.name: Heid
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Dieter, Eckhart, Gerd, Gotthilf, Heinz, Klaus, Konrad, Kurt, Manfred, Ralf, Uli.
German, Swiss German, and Jewish (Ashkenazic): topographic name from Middle High German heide, German Heide ‘heath, moor, open, uncultivated land’. Compare Heath .
German: from the ancient Germanic personal name Haido, a short form of any of various compound names beginning with haid ‘heath’ (i.e. wasteland, often with heather).

variants.name: Heyde
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Achim, Dietrich, Helmut, Volker.
German: topographic name for someone who lived on a heath, unprotected, open country, a variant of   Heide  .

variants.name: Hiday
meanings: Perhaps an Americanized form of German   Heide  .

nameMeanings.name: Robert
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the many French names of Germanic origin that were introduced into Britain by the Normans; it has since remained in continuous use. It is derived from the nearly synonymous elements hrōd ‘fame’ + berht ‘bright, famous’, and had a native Old English predecessor of similar form (Hreodbeorht), which was supplanted by the Norman name. Two dukes of Normandy in the 11th century bore the name: the father of William the Conqueror (sometimes identified with the legendary Robert the Devil), and his eldest son. It was borne also by three kings of Scotland, notably Robert the Bruce ( 1274–1329 ), who freed Scotland from English domination. The altered short form Bob is very common, but Hob and Dob, which were common in the Middle Ages and gave rise to surnames, are extinct. See also Rupert .

en.lifeSummary: When Robert Hedde I was born in 1420, in St Sepulchre Newgate, London, England, United Kingdom, his father, Richard Hedde, was 21 and his mother, Eunice Fanning, was 20. He married Margaret de Davenport in 1448, in St Sepulchre Newgate, London, England, United Kingdom. They were the parents of at least 10 sons and 2 daughters. He died in 1480, in London, England, United Kingdom, at the age of 60.
